coredata.dc:title: Heating performance of a large-scale line heater by adding synthesized carbon- nanodots to the heater bath fluid: CFD simulation and experimental study
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2023 The Society of Powder Technology Japan. Published by Elsevier B.V. and The Society of Powder Technology Japan. All rights reserved.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Society of Powder Technology Japan. Published by Elsevier B.V. and The Society of Powder Technology Japan.
coredata.dc:creator: Sadeghalvaad, Mehran
Reza Razavi, Seyyed
Sabbaghi, Samad
Rasouli, Kamal
coredata.dc:description: Numerous researchers have recently become interested in nanofluids because of their potential applications in thermal systems. Herein, various concentrations of nanofluid of synthesized carbon nanodots (C-dots) were used to survey the thermal performance of a line heater as a heater bath fluid, and a numerical model was proposed to simulate all of the line heater's components for the first time. C-dots nanoparticles were manufactured with the aid of the hydrothermal method and characterized using FTIR and XRD analysis. In addition, a two-stage approach was used to prepare the nanofluid with a varying concentration of C-dot nanoparticles, ranging from 0.5 to 5 wt%. Nanofluids were also measured and compared to the base fluid in terms of thermophysical properties. Additionally, a numerical model based on the finite element method (FEM) was proposed to simulate fluid flow and heat transfer in the line heater, and validation of the numerical results revealed good agreement with earlier studies. The mesh independence analysis shows that the results are much better and more consistent when mesh number 98,946 is used instead of the others. The results illustrate that temperatures were raised from roughly 300 K near the gas tubes to around 370 K near the fire tubes. In comparison to the situation of employing water as the bath fluid, the maximum heater power consumptions derived from the experiments and numerical findings were 9.7 % and 10.3 %, respectively.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon nanodots
CFD
Nanofluid
Line heater
Thermal conductivity

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Effects of C-dots on thermal performance of heater
Ethics approval and consent to participate

xocs:cp-notices.xocs:cp-notice: © 2023 The Society of Powder Technology Japan. Published by Elsevier B.V. and The Society of Powder Technology Japan. All rights reserved.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic of the process of fabricating stable nanofluid samples [27].
Geometry and meshing of the line heater.
FTIR spectrum (a), XRD analysis (b) and particle size distribution (c) of synthesized C-dots.
The effect of ultra-sonication time (a) and Surfactants (b, c) on the sedimentation % of NPs in C-dots/DW nanofluids.
Density (a), Dynamic viscosity (b), and specific heat capacity (c) of C-dots/DW nanofluids (C is degree celcius).

The effect of temperature on the thermal conductivity (a) and thermal conductivity enhancement (b) of the nanofluids, changing the PH and its effect on the thermal conductivities of the nanofluids (c).
Cross sectional velocity profiles in the fire tubes (a), gas tubes (b), and heater bath (c, d) for water, as the bath fluid.
Pressure contours in the internal domain (a, b), gas tubes (c), and fire tubes (b) for water, as the bath fluid.
Temperature profile (a), cross sectional temperature profile (b), 3D isothermal contours (c), and front view isothermal contours (d) of the system for water, as the bath fluid.
Contours (a) and Isothermal contours (b) of the system for the two case of using water and nanofluid, as the bath fluid.
Peaks of C-dots and their corresponding bonds.
Comparison of current data with previous studies on the thermophysical properties.

head.ce:title: Heating performance of a large-scale line heater by adding synthesized carbon- nanodots to the heater bath fluid: CFD simulation and experimental study

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Radin Faradid Petrosanat Co., Gas and Petroleum Department, Fars Science and Technology Park, Shiraz, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Radin Faradid Petrosanat Co., Gas and Petroleum Department, Fars Science and Technology Park, Shiraz, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemical and Petroleum Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Chemical Engineering Department, School of Chemical and Petroleum Engineering, Shiraz University, Shiraz, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Numerous researchers have recently become interested in nanofluids because of their potential applications in thermal systems. Herein, various concentrations of nanofluid of synthesized carbon nanodots (C-dots) were used to survey the thermal performance of a line heater as a heater bath fluid, and a numerical model was proposed to simulate all of the line heater's components for the first time. C-dots nanoparticles were manufactured with the aid of the hydrothermal method and characterized using FTIR and XRD analysis. In addition, a two-stage approach was used to prepare the nanofluid with a varying concentration of C-dot nanoparticles, ranging from 0.5 to 5 wt%. Nanofluids were also measured and compared to the base fluid in terms of thermophysical properties. Additionally, a numerical model based on the finite element method (FEM) was proposed to simulate fluid flow and heat transfer in the line heater, and validation of the numerical results revealed good agreement with earlier studies. The mesh independence analysis shows that the results are much better and more consistent when mesh number 98,946 is used instead of the others. The results illustrate that temperatures were raised from roughly 300 K near the gas tubes to around 370 K near the fire tubes. In comparison to the situation of employing water as the bath fluid, the maximum heater power consumptions derived from the experiments and numerical findings were 9.7 % and 10.3 %, respectively.

ce:section.ce:para: Natural gas (NG) pressure should be reduced from around 1000 psi in transit pipelines to 250 psi at city gas stations. In pressure reduction stations (PRS), a throttling valve or a regulator lowers the pressure [1]. On the other hand, the temperature of NG decreases when the pressure at the PRS drops because of the positive value of the NG Joule-Thomson coefficient. The production of gas hydrate is caused by temperature dips in pipes, and this phenomenon destroys equipment and disrupts system performance. Hence, a pre-heating system is required to warm the NG before to the PRS in order to maintain an acceptable temperature and minimize gas hydrate development in the pipelines [1]. The line heater (also known as an indirect water bath heater) is the most prevalent pre-heating device in the PRS, and it is made up of three parts: fire tubes, gas tubes, and bath fluid (shell side filled with water). Utilizing natural convection, fire tube coils heat the water that is used as the bath fluid to roughly 70 °C. The heater's heat harvesting process is completed with the aid of heating the gas tubes, which are already surrounded by the heated bath liquid [1,2]. The high operating costs of line heaters are due to their high fuel consumption and low efficiency [3]. As will be described in the following paragraphs, many efforts have been made to improve thermal efficiency and reduce fuel, energy, and greenhouse gas emissions in line heaters. To improve thermal efficiency and reduce the fossil fuel consumption of a PRS, an additional solar system has been proposed [4–7]. In a previous study, a single screw expander was used instead of a choke valve to reduce power consumption on the PRS [8,9]. However, Borelli et al. proposed gas pressure reduction in two stages through two turbo-expanders. They concluded that less energy was used in the designed system compared to traditional single-stage pressure reduction processes [10]. Some researchers focused on the twisted arrangement of the heating coil in laminar and turbulent flow regimes to improve the thermal efficiency of the bath fluid [11–14]. Angelo and his colleagues proposed using closed-loop, two-phase thermosiphon technology to replace the present heater. They reached the conclusion that the new system would not only increase the bath liquid's heat transmission coefficient but would also eliminate the problem of refilling. However, the main issues with their suggested solution were equipment degradation and gas leaks [15]. To establish the minimal preheating gas temperature, Olfati et al. modeled the regulator's operation. They concluded that the new system, when operated under optimal conditions, reduced energy consumption and destruction by 33 % and 15 %, respectively [16]. Azizi et al. proposed reusing the exhaust gas from the heat exchanger to recover the heat lost from the exhaust gas while improving the thermal efficiency of the heaters [17]. As a result, researchers in the literature were interested in redesigning the line heater to conserve energy and enhance the heater's thermal efficiency [18,19]. So, to reduce power consumption and improve heat transfer in line heaters, a new technique and solution are required. Nanotechnology is now widely used in many disciplines of engineering [20,21], including heating devices [22–28]. The nanofluid branch of nanotechnology was first defined by Choi in 1995, by dispersing nanoparticles (NPs) with the size of 1–100 nm to the base fluid [29]. Thereafter, different NPs of metal [30,31], non-metal [32,33], metal oxide [34–36], and ceramics [37,38] were used to be added to the base fluid to prepare nanofluids with enhanced heating capabilities. Many nanofluids were used to improve the thermal performance of heating devices [39,40]. Recent advances in modeling and simulation of nanofluid flows were discussed by Mahian et. al [41]. The ability of carbon nano-additives to increase heat transmission in the working fluid has already been demonstrated [42,43]. Carbon nanodots (C-dots) are a new type of carbon NPs with diameters smaller than 10 nm that have sparked interest due to their high-water solubility, chemical inertness, low toxicity, abundance, and low cost [44,45]. Many researchers have attempted to examine the production, characteristics, and uses of C-dots since 2004, when they were first obtained [46–49].

They have a wide range of uses in the domains of materials research, synthetic chemistry, biosensors, drug delivery, and bioimaging. These nanostructures exhibit great biocompatibility, are photochemically and physiochemically stable, have tunable fluorescence emission and excitation and are water soluble with low toxicities and inexpensive cost of production. As a result, there has been a lot of interest in the creation, characteristics, and applications of C - dots. Future research will focus on the creation of carbon dots with different morphologies, sizes, and target specificities. The poor thermal conductivity of traditional heat transfer fluids (water, air, ethylene glycol (EG), and engine oil) is one of the major challenges in greater heat transfer applications in engineering processes. Additionally, researchers are currently looking for ways to increase the speed and power of equipment like heat exchangers, car engines, and computer processors, as well as to minimize their size. Therefore, it is really important to provide a type of nanoparticle that can make fluids better at transferring heat and reduce the size of equipment [50].In order to assess the thermo-physical properties of nanofluids, a variety of additions, including metals, metal oxides, and carbon-based nanostructures (carbon nanotubes, graphene), have been reported. Metal and metal oxide have problems with sedimentation in the base fluid as their main drawbacks, but carbon-based nanostructures have functionalized groups that allow them to switch from a hydrophobic to a hydrophilic shape. Additionally, the use of green carbon nanoparticles can only slightly minimize environmental concerns regarding the potential toxicity of metal and metal oxide nanoparticles.

The water-based graphene quantum dots nanofluids were created as a stable suspension by Amiri et al. The thermophysical and rheological properties of their nanofluid samples were tested, and it was determined that the samples have superior colloidal stabilities and thermal conductivities, while the nanofluids' densities and viscosities did not change considerably [51]. Another study used graphene quantum dots dispersed in car radiator coolant as the base fluid to create low-cost, environmentally friendly, and high-stability nanofluids with thermal conductivity and heat transfer coefficients that were higher than the base fluid's, which were 5.2 % and 17 %, respectively [52].

Nanofluids with improved thermal performance could be used instead of water in-line heater bath fluids. The first researchers to propose employing nanofluids instead of water in line heaters were Rahmati and Reiszadeh. To make the nanofluids with higher thermal capacities, they dispersed multi-walled carbon nanotubes (MWCNTs) in varying quantities in the base fluid of ethylene–glycol/water. The nanofluid samples were then used in an experimental line heater, with a maximum thermal efficiency enhancement of 48 % when the heater bath fluid was made up of 5 vol% MWCNTs/ethylene glycol and water [42]. It makes sense to investigate the effects of adding new nano-additives to the bath fluid on the heating performance and energy consumption of the line heater.

In current work, for the first time, the impacts of different concentrations of nanofluid of C-dot nanoparticles synthesized on the thermophysical properties of the base fluid, as well as the line heater's power consumption reduction, were explored, and a numerical model was proposed to simulate all of the line heater's components.

Ethanol, Hydrogen peroxide (H2O2– 30 wt%), deionized water, and pure water were purchased from Sigma Aldrich Company for hydrothermal synthesis of C-dots. HCL and NaOH were used in order to adjust the pH of the reaction (control protonation of carboxyl groups), also provided by Sigma Aldrich Company. In addition, surfactants of Sodium dodecyl sulfate (SDS), Sodium dodecylbenzene sulfonate (SDBS), and Hexadecyl trimethyl ammonium bromide (HCTAB) were purchased from Merck Millipore Company, these surfactants were used to control the stability of nanoparticles in a nanofluid. It is to be noted that ethanol is one of the most common laboratory reagents, used as a low-cost and abundant carbon source in order to manufacture carbon nanotubes by catalytic chemical vapor deposition. In recent work, ethanol is hydrothermally oxidized with the environmentally friendly oxidant hydrogen peroxide to create highly fluorescent C-dots in a single step. This approach is more economical and environmentally friendly than other approaches because it only needs the most accessible and safe reagents, ethanol and H2O2, and it doesn't need expensive equipment, harsh synthetic conditions, or laborious procedures. The achieving C-dots have lots of oxygen functional groups and are highly crystalline.

In the current study, ethanol is hydrothermally oxidized using the ecologically benign oxidant hydrogen peroxide to create extremely luminous C-dots in a single step. This approach is more economical and environmentally friendly than other approaches because it only needs the most accessible and safe reagents, ethanol and H2O2, and it doesn't need expensive equipment, harsh synthetic conditions, or laborious procedures. The resulting C-dots have lots of oxygen functional groups and are highly crystalline. It has been found that H2O2 is necessary for the conversion of ethanol to CO, when ethanol is treated hydrothermally in the presence of 1 % H2O2, demonstrating the formation of C-dots. Furthermore, as the concentration of H2O2 increases from 1 to 5 wt%, the number of C-dots produced increases.But as the H2O2 concentration is increased from 5 to 8 wt%, the yield of C-dots falls. It seems that an excessively high H2O2 concentration causes ethanol to overly oxidize into CO2 and H2O, lowering the yield of C-dots.

C-dot nanoparticles were produced by adding ethanol in an H2O2 solution (30 wt%) with the aid of the hydrothermal technique [53]. For this purpose, in a 50 mL Teflon-lined, stainless-steel autoclave with 4 mL commercial hydrogen peroxide and 23 mL deionized water, 3 mL ethanol was added. The final H2O2 content in the 30 mL solution was 4 wt%. The hydrothermal approach of the produced solution at 180 °C for 12 h causes a yellow dispersion of C-dots. Finally, approximately 5 mg of C-dot powder was achieved after the C-dots were purified with the help of a dialysis tube (molecular weight cut off: 100–500 Da) and the solvent was evaporated in an oven at 60 °C. It should be emphasized that, for the synthesis of C-dots, the reaction temperature is additionally crucial. Indeed, no C-dot produces heat below 120 °C, when the reaction temperature is raised from 140 to 180 °C, it is shown that the carbonization of C-dots is facilitated and a better surface structure is produced.

Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy of the synthesized C-dots was obtained with the aid of an FTIR spectrometer (Thermo Scientific Nicolet iS5) using pressed KBr flakes (including 1 % specimen) in the wavenumber range of 400–4000 cm−1 and in a temperature room in order to determine the functional groups of C-dots manufactured. Additionally, the X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern of the NPs was already recorded with the assistance of a Bruker D8 advanced diffractometer, which was equipped with K-Cu radiation at the rate of 1°/min. It is to be noted that the X-ray diffraction (XRD) technique was used to identify and confirm the material structure and crystalline phase of synthesized C-dots.

First of all, various different amounts of C-dots, ranging from 0.5 to 5 wt%, were dispersed in DW with the aid of two-stage approaches, including magnetic stirring and ultrasound dispersion (Considering the procedure used in [54,55]). It is to be noted that the concentration of nanofluid was chosen considering pre-experiments. It is well known that there is a clearly recognizable relationship between the sediment of nanoparticles and the concentration of nanofluid. The more concentration increases, the more rises thermal conductivity property. It is important to note that in order to improve the dispersion of NPs in DW, a two-stage method was used. Afterward, an analytical balance (model ABT-5 M, Kern Corporation) with a measurement range of 10 mg–200 g and a maximum error of 0.1 mg was utilized for the massing of the synthesized NPs. The first step of fabricating nanofluids was to disperse the NPs into the DW with a magnetic stirrer for an hour at ambient temperature and atmospheric pressure. The second step was to use a half-industrial ultrasonic vibrator (model UIP500hd, Hielscher, power consumption of 500 W and frequency of 20 kHz, Germany) at different time durations of ultra-sonication to have C-dots/DW nanofluids with various concentrations of NPs. The flow model of the ultra-sonic processor was used due to the need for high volumes of nanofluids. To prevent the sedimentation of NPs and to have stability in the prepared solution samples for a specific time duration, the effects of ultra-sonication time and additives of surfactants were counted on the nanofluid stabilities. For this purpose, nanofluid samples were imposed on the ultrasonic waves for time durations of 0, 15, 30, 60, 90, 120, and 180 min, and the effects of adding three surfactants of SDS, SDBS, and HCTAB to the C-dots/DW nanofluids (with 0.1 and 1 wt% of NP content) were investigated. It is important to stress that all the other experiments in this study were conducted based on the optimum state of stability. Fig. 1 shows a process schematic for fabricating stable nanofluids.

Viscosity measurements of the nanofluid samples were done by a SVMTM series viscometer (SVMTM 2001, Anton Paar, Austria) with a kinematic measurement range of 0.2 mm2 s−1 to 30000 mm2 s−1. A precise density meter (Model DMA 4500 M, Anton Paar, Austria) with an accuracy of 0.00005 g cm−3 was used to record nanofluid density. A Setaram Micro DSC II microcalorimeter was also utilized to measure the specific heat capacities of nanofluids based on the scanning mode method. The design of experimental calorimetric batch vessels (with a volume of 1 cm3) was done in such a way as to avoid leakage from the samples. Finally, the thermal conductivity of the samples was measured with the transient hot-wire method by the KD2 Thermal Analyzer (Decagon Devices, USA) with an accuracy of 0.01 W m−1 K−1 and was based on the standard methods of ASTM D5334 and IEEE 442–1981. Indeed, it is made up of sensors that can be put into the medium you want to measure and a handheld controller. While dual-needle sensors also measure volumetric specific heat capacity and diffusivity, single-needle sensors only measure thermal conductivity and resistivity. Typically, a single reading takes two minutes. The probe is heated for 30 s with a specified amount of current after the initial 90 s are utilized to assure temperature stability. A thermistor that measures the changing temperature is also a part of the probe, and the microprocessor saves the information. The temperature difference verse time data is used to calculate the fluid's thermal conductivity at the conclusion of the reading. The references provide more details on the method's underlying theory [56–58]. The fluid specimen is put inside a test tube to measure thermal conductivity at ambient temperature. The fluid's thermal conductivity is then measured when the apparatus probe is firmly inserted into the tube. This article's main goal is to determine the temperature-dependent values of the thermal conductivity of nanofluids; therefore, the fluid specimen is placed inside a heated, insulated cage (see [59]).

The thermal conductivities of nanofluids were investigated in terms of NP content and temperature. In addition, alkaline agents of NaOH and HCl were added to the nanofluid samples to study the change of pH on the thermal conductivities. Some correlations for the prediction of the properties of nanofluids are presented below (nf and bf refer to nanofluid and base fluid, respectively).

Where ks and kbs are the thermal conductivities of nanoparticles and the base fluid, respectively. To predict the thermal conductivity of nanofluid [60], the Maxwell model was applied.

Specific heat capacity (Cpnf) can also be calculated by Equation (5), which was suggested in the literature [61].

Prepared nanofluids were considered to be utilized in a real and large-scale line heater (Fig. 2 ). This heater, which is a pre-heating system for avoiding the gas temperature fall and the formation of hydrate crystals in the gas stream, is located in the natural gas pressure drop stations, and the main parts of the system are fire-tubes, gas tubes, and the heater shell (also called heater bath). In general, fire tube coils warm the water (or nanofluid) on the shell side to transfer heat to the gas tubes. The heat-gaining process will be continued, and the gas temperature should be kept at 38C to prevent hydrate formation in the gas transportation pipelines [2]. One of the methods for improving the heat performance of the heater and decreasing its power consumption is to improve the thermophysical properties of the water bath with nano-additives [42]. C-dots/DW nanofluids with two NP contents (0.5 and 5 wt%) were applied to be replaced with the water in the heater shell to evaluate the thermal performance and power consumption of the heater by using C-dots. The heater’s power was set manually. As the outlet gas temperature reached 38 °C, the power started recording.

A computational fluid dynamic (CFD) model based on the FEM was proposed to simulate the hydrodynamic and heating performance of a line heater. It is important to stress that the application of the finite element method covers from field analysis of heat flux, fluid flow, magnetic flux, and other flow problems to deformation and stress analysis of automotive and bridge structures. The fact that the finite element approach is a fairly generic technique is one factor in its effectiveness in multi-physics analysis. The finite element method technique is applied to reduce the continuous spatial solution into a finite number of time-dependent variables. Furthermore, in some cases where higher-order accuracy is required, FEM is really successful, and it plays a crucial role in the quality of the mesh. The model geometry consists of a heater shell, along with gas and fire tubes, and was fabricated in the COMSOL Multiphysics 5.3 software. Four rows of gas tubes (2.4 m length and 3-inch diameter) and two rows of fire tubes (2.7 m length and 10-inch diameter) were designed to be located in the heater shell. The distance of the tubes to the heater walls was also considered to be the same as the main dimensions of the heater. All materials were considered to be carbon steel. A set of partial differential equations (PDEs) for the estimation of state variables (velocity, pressure, temperature, and turbulence variables) in an infinite number of tiny fluid particles were considered to be included in the CFD model.PDEs were then converted to matrixes of linear algebraic equations and solved by the numerical technique of FEM, using mixed unstructured grids containing tetrahedral, pyramid, prism, triangle, and quad grids in COMSOL software. The use of 98,946 grids for the domain resulted in acceptable and consistent results. For turbulent gas flow in gas tubes, laminar fluid flow in heater shells, and turbulent fuel flow in fire tubes, transport equations were solved in discrete steps utilizing multi-frontal massively parallel sparse direct values. The convergence criterion for maximum residuals between two iterations was 1 × 10-3. Fig. 3 shows the schematic geometry and meshing of the line heater. The bar chart in the mesh picture is for the quality of meshing. As can be seen in the picture, mesh quality ranges from 0 to 1, and the average mesh quality was reported to be 58 %.The meshing of regions near the walls was done with as high a mesh quality as possible because the meshing of these regions has the most influence on the solution. This meshing was done by considering two parameters: firstly, it reduces discretization error by increasing mesh quality, and, secondly, it reaches convergence.

The Navier-Stokes equations of continuity, momentum, and energy were applied to simulate the fluid flow and heat transfer in the whole system of the line heater. Turbulence is the fluid flow regime in the gas and fire tubes, and the standard K- model, a Reynolds Average Navier Stokes (RANS) turbulent model, was used to model turbulent flow in these sections. However, the flow of fluid in the shell side of the system was considered to be laminar, and natural convection heat transfer is dominant in this region. As a result, equations (6), 8–12 were solved for the gas and fire tubes separately, and equations (6), 7, and 10 were considered to model the natural flow of fluid (water or nanofluid) on the shell side (water bath) [62]. Parameters of the turbulence model containing k (turbulent kinetic energy), (turbulent dissipation rate), along with P (pressure), are calculated using Equations 5–7, respectively.

, Q, ε, pk, k, V, and K are density, temperature, velocity vector, specific heat capacity, dynamic viscosity, pressure, identity matrix of [3 * 3], the total force acting per volume, turbulence dynamic viscosity, heat source, the turbulence dissipation rate, turbulent kinetic pressure, heat conductivity, volume, and the turbulence kinetic energy respectively. Pref and Tref are equal to 1 atm and 293.15 k, respectively and
ρ

f
in equation (2) will be determined from these values [62].

According to the ambient temperature in the winter and gas pressure drop laws, the gas temperature inlet and outlet were 15 °C and 38 °C, respectively. The gas inlet flux was 10000 m3/h, and the pressure inlet was also 250 psi, while the pressure drop was consumed to be neglected in the system. A no-slip condition in the walls was considered, and wall functions were assumed to model the fluid behavior near the walls. fluid (water or nanofluid) was assumed to be Newtonian and incompressible. Laminar natural convection heat transfer equations were solved for them. The bath fluid velocity inlet was zero and its outlet pressure was already zero. The system was in a steady-state condition. Thermophysical properties of the gas, fuel and bath fluid were temperature-dependent, and for the nanofluid samples, measured thermophysical properties from the experimental results were applied. Superheated steam was considered as the fuel in the fire tubes, and the fuel inlet velocity and temperature were 4.5 m/s and 350 k, respectively, while the fuel outlet pressure was zero. Furthermore, a thin layer condition was assumed for the fire tube coils with a layer thickness of 0.005 m and a thermal conductivity of 40 W/m.K, respectively. Thermal insulation conditions were considered for the whole pre-heating system.

The chemical structure (surface functional groups) of the synthesized NPs was investigated with the help of FTIR spectroscopy. Fig. 4 (a) shows the FTIR spectrum of manufactured C-dots and index peaks are clearly shown to identify C-dots. The creation of carbon structures in the hydrothermal synthesis of C-dots is clearly shown in Table 1 , which depicts the peaks of the C-dots index and their corresponding bonds. In general, the results demonstrate that C-dot NPs were successfully synthesized.

The crystal structure and phase purity of synthesized NPs were analyzed with the assistance of XRD. XRD analysis of C-dots showed that the peak of 23.1° is related to the carbon structure of the graphite (Fig. 4 b) and this result does not contradict the findings of the literature [63]. Chen et al. indicated that the XRD analysis of C-dots has a main peak around 23° [64]. It can be concluded from the results of FTIR and XRD analysis that C-dots were synthesized successfully. In addition, particle size distribution analysis of C-dots revealed that they are nanometer (2–9 nm) in size, with approximately 35 % of the synthesized C-dots having a particle size of 4 nm (Fig. 4(c)).
•
Particle distribution in nanofluids

To determine the particle distribution in the solution samples containing particles in the range of micro and nano sizes, a dynamic light scattering approach was applied. In other words, a particle size analysis (PSA) experiment was used to measure the size of manufactured NPs with the help of HORIBA DLS Version LB-550. Fig. 5 indicates the distribution of the particle in C-dots/DW nanofluid with NPs of the average size of approximately 4.8 nm. The results of this experiment not only showed the nano-scale size of Synthesized C-dots but also of nanofluid samples' well-formation.
•
Stability of the nanofluids

First of all, it should be noted that the stability of nanofluids was evaluated with the aid of sedimentation tests. Actually, after preparing various concentrations of nanofluids, they were left in ambient conditions without being moved, and after some time, the percentage of particle aggregation was assessed. Finally, the fewer nanoparticles that aggregate, the more stable the nanofluid is. Despite C-dots' excellent water solubility, sedimentation and agglomeration of high NP concentrations in the solutions were unavoidable. Placement of samples under ultrasound waves and increasing the time of ultrasonication, on the other hand, resulted in higher stability in the samples after only one month. Different ultra-sonication times were tested on the samples to examine the impacts of ultra-sonication time on the stability of various concentrations of nanofluids (1.5, 3, and 5 wt%). The results showed that 30 min of ultrasound was the best time for nanofluid stability (Fig. 6 (a)). It may be deduced that the longer the ultrasonication time is prolonged from 30 min to 120 min, the more the formed bonds fracture [65].

Figs. 6 and 7 show the effects of adding SDS, SDBS, and HCTAB surfactants to nanofluids containing 0.1 and 1 wt% C-dots after 6 weeks (b and c). The surfactant from SDBS was found to have the greatest performance of the three in terms of enhancing nanofluid stability and decreasing NP sedimentation rates in the samples. Furthermore, the optimal NP to surfactant content ratio was discovered to be 1:10. As a result, the optimum stability performance for C-dots/DW nanofluid samples was obtained after 90 min of ultrasonication and the usage of SDBS with a content ratio of 1:10. This impact of surfactants could be attributed to the interactions of molecules between C-dots nanoparticles and the surfactant's hydrophilic and hydrophobic filaments, as well as the electrostatic force between them and shortage of particle aggregation, which could be owing to strong repulsive forces between nanoparticles.
•
Analysis of thermophysical properties of nanofluids

The densities, viscosities, and specific heat capacities of nanofluids containing 1.5, 3, and 5 % C-dots were measured, as well as the impact of temperature on these parameters (Fig. 7). In addition, all of the nanofluid samples were tested for thermal conductivities and the effects of NP concentration, temperature, and pH on thermal conductivity changes (Fig. 8 ). All of the experiments were repeated three times, with the mean values being used in the graphs.

The inclusion of C-dots at ambient temperature resulted in increasing DW density, according to the findings. The density of samples was increased when the NP concentration was increased to 5 wt%. Temperatures of the samples were elevated to 50 and 80°Celsius to investigate how their densities changed. The samples' densities were reduced by raising their temperatures even higher, because the higher the temperature, the higher the solubility and dispersion. Adding C-dots to DW also caused its dynamic viscosity to be increased and raising the NP content already increased the dynamic viscosity. The dynamic viscosity of DW was increased by adding C-dots, while the dynamic viscosity had already been raised by increasing the NP content. The slope of the dynamic viscosity rise becomes almost constant when the NP concentration is greater than 3 wt%. The increased shear stress and apparent viscosity of the nanofluids versus their shear speeds when compared to the DW can explain this. The nanofluid samples are non-Newtonian, and 5 wt% C-dots/DW nanofluid yielded the highest apparent viscosity, shear stress, and dynamic viscosity. Furthermore, increasing the temperature reduced the dynamic viscosities of the samples. The specific heat capacity measurements for all of the samples revealed that increasing both the NP concentration in the nanofluids and the temperature decreased specific heat capacities. If the NPs and DW are in thermal equilibrium, the first rule of thermodynamics can be used to define the specific heat capacity of the nanofluids.

The thermal conductivities of C-dots/DW nanofluids at various temperatures and NP weight percent are shown in Fig. 8 (a). The thermal conductivities of the samples clearly increased as the temperature was increased to 80 °C. Indeed, at 80 °C, the thermal conductivity of 5 wt% C-dots/DW nanofluid was 0.73 W/m.K, the highest of all the thermal conductivities measured. Adding up to 5 wt% C-dots to the DW enhanced the thermal conductivities of the resultant nanofluid samples by up to 5.8 %–8.9 % compared to the DW under the same conditions (Fig. 8 (b)). The samples' stability and, as a result, their thermal conductivities could be affected by changes in their PH (Fig. 8(c)). As a result, the addition of HCl and NaOH changed the pH of the nanofluid samples. Indeed, by increasing the PH to a certain level, the thermal conductivities of all of the nanofluids were improved. The ideal pH for C-dots/DW nanofluid with the most stable and maximum thermal conductivity was discovered to be between 7 and 8. However, increasing the NP concentration of the nanofluids tends to increase this optimal pH range.

Table 2 compares the current data with previous studies on thermophysical properties. It is important to stress that even though all of the nanofluids provide a high potential for improving the thermophysical properties of base fluid, carbon nanodots show a lower viscosity in comparison with the others which can be really significant in many different applications. Furthermore, the features that make carbon- nanodots are just unique from their kind are nothing but their affordability and environmental friendliness. In general, carbon nanodots can be successful in enhancing the thermophysical characteristics of base fluids.

Profiles of velocity, pressure, and temperature were calculated with the aid of FEM to solve the governing equations of fluids in the line heater. In addition, the impact of adding C-dots to the heater bath on the heater's power consumption was studied.

The cross-sectional velocity profile of fuel in fire tubes is shown in Fig. 11 (a), and it can be seen that the velocity of fuel in fire tubes increases from the inlet to the end of the first row and reduces at the joint section. The fuel velocity increases in the second row of fire tubes until it reaches the mid-point of the tube, then lowers at the exit section. The gas velocity profile in the gas tubes is already shown in Fig. 11 (b), and it can be seen that the gas velocity is highest at the inlet section of the fire tubes and decreases in subsequent tube rows due to friction generated by the joints. The gas velocity is raised at the end of each row of gas tubes, as well as at the outlet. Despite the fact that the output gas velocity was somewhat lower than the inlet, the gas velocity change range in each row of the gas tubes was only around 0.05 m/s, which is negligible. It's worth noting that the biggest velocity reductions occurred in the first and second rows of gas tubes, with the velocity steadily increasing in the subsequent rows. Because of the no-slip criterion that was addressed in these parts, gas velocity near the wall was already reduced. The cross-sectional bath fluid velocity profile between the gas and fire tubes is also shown in Fig. 11 (c). Bath fluid velocity was slightly higher near fire tubes than in other parts of the domain, and it was raised further in these locations until it reached the system's outlet section, creating more turbulence and, as a result, more heat transfer in these areas than in other parts of the system. Fig. 11 (d) shows the system's last cross-section bath fluid velocity, as well as several high-velocity spots at the joints and above the gas walls.

One of the important parameters that were obtained from the numerical model was the system pressure. As a result, gas pressure drop in the gas tubes was assumed to be neglected, and as a result, pressure changes in the system were not considerable. However, investigating this parameter in the regions near the gas and fire tubes along with the bath fluid is important to have a good understanding of the low and high-pressure regions of the system. The pressure contours of the system are shown in Fig. 12 (a, b). The intake gas pressure is high because the gas inlet section is imposed at the greatest pressure (Fig. 12(c)).

Gas tube joints had also been subjected to higher pressures than the rest of the system. The pressures in the entrance section of the fire tubes were lower than the pressures in the regions surrounding the joints and outlet portions of the fire tubes, which increased and dropped, respectively (Fig. d).

The most significant characteristic of a modeling system is temperature, and temperature distribution depicts heat transmission throughout the system. The heat transfer from the fire tubes to the bath fluid visibly warms the gas tubes, as seen in Fig. 13 (a-c). This heating-up process also has the goal of maintaining the system's gas temperature at a constant level. As a result, the fire tube coils must have certain power consumption findings show that the regions near the fire tubes have greater temperatures (average temperatures of 350–370 K) than the other locations, indicating that heat can be transported to the entire system. Roughly gas tubes, the usual temperature is around 300–310 K. As the heater's power consumption rises, the bath fluid temperature rises as well, causing the gas temperature in the gas tubes to rise Fig. 13 (c, d) depicts isothermal contours, which are zones with constant temperatures. Gas inlet parts have lower temperatures, whereas those near the fire tube coils have higher temperatures. The temperature distributions throughout the entire system are shown in all four of Fig. 13 (a-d).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of C-dots on thermal performance of heater
ce:section.ce:para: The created nanofluids can reduce the heater's power consumption and limit its fuel usage, hence choosing a suitable bath fluid with higher heat transfer capacities than water is advised. As previously stated, the effect of nanofluid thermophysical properties on the thermal performance of the system was investigated, and as shown in Fig. 14 (a and b), the high-temperature regions near the fire tube coils were reduced when a 5 wt% C-dots/DW nanofluid was used as the bath fluid rather than water. This means that less energy and fuel are used to reach a certain gas temperature, and more heat is distributed throughout the system. The heat transmission of fluid to the gas tubes increased as the thermal conductivity of the nanofluid increased (which is the aim of this work). The heat transfer increment rises as the concentration of NPs rises, raising the gas outlet temperature in the pipes. The use of C-dots in water has the advantage of preventing NPs from settling in the fluid and system. Furthermore, chaotic NP movement in the fluid boosts heat transmission and energy exchange between the fluid and tube walls, warming the gas in the gas tubes. The number of contacts and collisions among particles grows as the NP concentration in the fluid rises. To put it another way, increasing the concentration of NPs aids in the intensification of heat transfer mechanisms in gas.

The hydrothermal approach was used to successfully create C-dot nanoparticles, which were then dispersed in water using two separate methods to prepare nanofluids. There have been a few studies in the literature looking into improving the bath fluid properties of line heaters, but none have looked into the effect of adding C-dots to the bath fluid on the heater's power consumption. The thermophysical properties of the produced nanofluids were investigated, and they were used as the bath fluid in a real-scale line heater. Considering Table 2, carbon nanodots can be successful in enhancing the thermophysical characteristics of base fluids. As a result, their stability was investigated, and it was determined that 90 min of ultrasonication and the use of SDBS surfactant with a 1:10 content ratio (surfactant to NP content) resulted in stable nanofluids with no sedimentation after one month. The greatest thermal conductivity enhancement over DW was found to be about 8.9 % for 5 wt% C-dots/DW nanofluid, and the optimal PH range to have maximum thermal conductivities in the range of 8–9. FEM was used to model the heating device, and the findings of the heater's power consumption with and without C-dots in the bath fluid were compared to the results of the experiments to ensure that the proposed model was valid. The velocity, pressure, and temperature distributions in the entire system were obtained and reported to gain a better knowledge of the line heater's hydrodynamic and thermal performance. The system's pressure shift was minor, and the pressure drop was close to zero. The mesh independence analysis shows that the results are much better and more consistent when mesh number 98,946 is used instead of the others. The temperature near the gas tubes may be approximately 300 K, while near the fire tubes it could be around 350–370 K. In the trials and numerical modeling, adding C-dots to the bath fluid lowered heater power consumption by 9.7 % and 10.3 %, respectively, when compared to using water as the bath fluid.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Ethics approval and consent to participate
ce:section.ce:para: We would like to emphasize that the submitted manuscript was prepared in accordance with the Authors' Guide to Manuscript Preparation and the Ethics in Publishing Policy. We confirm that keywords are provided and references are in the correct format.

We acknowledge that the organization and university with which we are affiliated are fully aware of our submission and that the manuscript's publication has been accepted by all authors as well as the responsible authorities in the area where the work was done.
Data and materials are included in the manuscript. However, further data and material will be provided as requested. The authors do not wish to share data.
This research received a grant from Fars Science and Technology Park with the contract number 720–3654-58352.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors are grateful to Fars Science and Technology Park for providing equipped laboratories for them to successfully complete this work.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.apt.2023.103960.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
